retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ectopic pregnancy, in essence, is the implantation of an embryo outside of the uterine cavity. Ectopic pregnancies most commonly occur in the fallopian tube (see Image . Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy); however, they may also develop within cesarean scars, the endocervical canal (see Image . Cervical Ectopic Pregnancy), the ovary, and the peritoneal cavity. Within the fallopian tube, ectopics most frequently are located at the ampulla region (70%), but may occur anywhere along the length of the tube, eg, the cornua (interstitial ectopic), fimbriae, or proximal segment (isthmic ectopic). [2] An ectopic pregnancy may also occur concomitantly with a normally developing pregnancy in the endometrial cavity, known as a heterotopic pregnancy. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Female Infertility -- Complications -- Ectopic Pregnancy
retrieved_docs.content: Ectopic pregnancy following treatment of infertile patients is another risk that requires extensive counseling. There is a two-to-threefold increase in ectopic pregnancies among infertility patients. This is thought to be associated with a high percentage of tubal factor infertility. [80] The highest associated risk of ectopic pregnancy is after tubal surgery to correct tubal factor infertility. Rates of ectopic pregnancy following tubal reconstructive surgery are approximately 9%, with other reports as high as 30%. [63] The risk of ectopic pregnancy with IVF fresh embryo transfer is higher than frozen embryo transfer, but the overall rate of ectopic pregnancies with IVF is roughly 1.3%. [81] There does not seem to be an increase in ectopic pregnancies with the use of ovulation induction agents combined with IUI versus natural conception; however, in a large study comparing the ovulation induction agents, clomiphene, letrozole, and gonadotropins had ectopic pregnancy rates of 4%, 6%, and 8%, respectively. [82]

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy, Ultrasound -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In the adnexal area, viewing the fallopian tubes and ovaries, there are multiple signs consistent with potential ectopic pregnancies. Simple adnexal cysts and the presence of positive beta hCG levels have approximately a 10% chance of being an ectopic pregnancy. A complex extra adnexal cyst or mass has a 95% chance of being a tubal ectopic if there is no intrauterine pregnancy identified. If this is within the adnexa, it is much more likely to be a corpus luteum ectopic pregnancy. Any solid mass in the adnexa may be ectopic but is not specific. A tubal ring sign is a sign of a tubal ectopic in which there is an echogenic ring surrounding a likely unruptured ectopic pregnancy, with a reported 95% positive predictive value.

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Etiology -- Ectopic Pregnancy Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Fallopian tube damage : Due to infections (gonorrhea, chlamydia), prior pelvic or tubal surgery, or endometriosis

retrieved_docs.title: Tubo-Ovarian Abscess -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: TOA presents serious risks, including infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and a heightened risk of ectopic pregnancy. A ruptured abscess can result in life-threatening sepsis, underscoring the need for prompt evaluation and treatment at the first clinical suspicion. [2] [3] [4] Initial management typically involves broad-spectrum antibiotics, which are effective in many cases. However, larger abscesses or those unresponsive to antibiotics within 72 hours may require surgical intervention or image-guided drainage. [5] Surgical approaches vary and include laparoscopy or laparotomy, with considerations based on factors such as abscess size, patient age, fertility considerations, and surgical history.

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilized egg implants outside the uterine cavity, a condition that affects approximately 1% to 2% of pregnancies in the United States. Ectopic pregnancy is a potentially life-threatening condition and accounts for 2.7% of pregnancy-related deaths. [1] Most ectopic pregnancies (approximately 97%) occur within the fallopian tube, commonly linked to underlying fallopian tube abnormalities. [2] [3] Such abnormalities may result from prior infections (eg, gonorrhea or chlamydia), tubal surgeries (including sterilization), prior ectopic pregnancies, or exposure to diethylstilbestrol in utero. Additional risk factors include conception while using intrauterine devices (IUDs) or progesterone-only contraceptives. [1] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: An upregulation of proinflammatory cytokines occurs following tubal damage; this subsequently promotes embryo implantation, invasion, and angiogenesis within the fallopian tube. [8] Chlamydia trachomatis infection results in the production of interleukin 1 by tubal epithelial cells; this is a vital indicator for embryo implantation within the endometrium. [8] Interleukin 1 also has a role in downstream neutrophil recruitment, which would further contribute to fallopian tubal damage. [8] Cilia beat frequency is also negatively affected by smoking and infection. Hormonal variations throughout the menstrual cycle additionally have demonstrated effects on cilia beat frequency. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, the various sites where ectopic pregnancy occurs have different incidences. Tubal ectopic pregnancies are the most common, with rupture rates around 15% in Western countries, a figure that may have increased during the COVID-19 pandemic. [8] Cervical ectopic pregnancy has an incidence of <1% and has been reported to occur following dilation and curettage in 70% of these patients. Ectopic pregnancies within the ovary occur in <3% of cases, while abdominal implantation occurs in 0.9% to 1.4% of cases. [4] Abdominal pregnancies have a higher mortality than other types of ectopic pregnancy at 10%; however, due to a higher frequency of delayed diagnosis and up to 7 times higher risk of organ perforation and massive hemorrhage. [4] Interstitial ectopic pregnancies are reported in up to 4% of all ectopic implantation sites. Reports also exist of implantation sites in omental, retroperitoneal, splenic, and hepatic locations. [4] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnoses to consider with ectopic pregnancies include: Ovarian torsion Tubo-ovarian abscess Appendicitis Hemorrhagic corpus luteum Ovarian cyst rupture Threatened miscarriage Incomplete miscarriage Pelvic inflammatory disease Ureteral calculi [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Management primarily aims to preserve fertility, improve diagnostic accuracy, and provide psychological support. Treatment varies, depending on clinical stability, ectopic location, beta-human chorionic gonadotropin (β-hCG) levels, and ultrasound findings ranging from expectant management to surgical interventions. In select cases, nonsurgical treatment with methotrexate may be effective, especially when pregnancies are diagnosed early and meet specific criteria. However, medical treatment is less likely to succeed in cases involving larger masses, high β-hCG levels, or visible embryos. Advanced or ruptured cases typically require urgent surgical intervention. [1] [4] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilized egg implants outside the uterus, most commonly in the fallopian tube. Prompt recognition of this condition is essential, as in the United States, approximately 1% to 2% of pregnancies are affected, and an estimated 2.7% of pregnancy-related deaths are caused by ectopic pregnancy rupture. Risk factors include pelvic inflammatory disease, smoking, prior ectopic pregnancy, fallopian tube surgery, and infertility. Symptoms typically include lower abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy, particularly if intrauterine pregnancy has not been confirmed.

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for ectopic pregnancy depends on early diagnosis and timely intervention. If identified and treated before rupture, either medically with methotrexate or surgically, the risk of severe complications is significantly reduced. However, delayed diagnosis can lead to tubal rupture, resulting in life-threatening hemorrhage and hemodynamic instability. In such cases, emergency surgical intervention is required, which may involve salpingectomy, potentially impacting future fertility. [4] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy, Ultrasound -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The common location of ectopic pregnancies differs between whether the patient had a natural conception or assisted reproductive technologies such as in vitro fertilization. In natural conception, 95% of the ectopic pregnancies will be tubal, 1.4% will be abdominal, and less than 1% will be cervical or ovarian. With assisted reproductive technologies, 82% of ectopic pregnancies will be tubal with a primary ampullary predominance. Approximately 11% of ectopic pregnancies after assisted reproductive technologies will be heterotopic, which is a vast difference from natural conception. Understanding these differences stresses the importance of obtaining a history of the method of conception.

retrieved_docs.title: Salpingitis Isthmica Nodosa -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: While salpingitis isthmica nodosa itself does not directly cause mortality, and it dramatically increases a patient’s risk of having an ectopic pregnancy, which has a mortality rate of 2% in the developing world and 0.2% in developed countries. [31] As previously described, the treatment of salpingitis isthmica nodosa is focused on restoring and maintaining fertility (IVF, GnRH-a’s, TCA, and interventional radiology interventions) and has improved significantly over the past couple of decades. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Ectopic Pregnancy
retrieved_docs.content: In general, the treatment of an ectopic pregnancy balances the management of the ectopic gestation with the aim of preserving a patient's reproductive capacity as much as possible, if desired. Therefore, interventions should be tailored to optimize the patient's preferences for future reproductive outcomes. [3] The patient's hemodynamic stability directs the initial management approach for an ectopic pregnancy. Any patient exhibiting signs of a ruptured ectopic pregnancy should be urgently transferred for surgical intervention. If the ectopic pregnancy is diagnosed early, the patient remains hemodynamically stable, and the affected fallopian tube is intact, treatment options include medical management with intramuscular methotrexate or surgical options, eg, salpingostomy (removal of the ectopic pregnancy while preserving the fallopian tube) or salpingectomy (removal of part or all of the affected fallopian tube). [3] The choice between medical and surgical management depends on clinical findings, ultrasound results, serum β-hCG levels, and patient preference.

retrieved_docs.title: Ectopic Pregnancy -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Findings of Non-Fallopian Tube Pregnancies
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical pregnancies, once typically diagnosed in the second trimester or at the time of spontaneous abortion, can now be detected earlier through TVUS. Although no standardized treatment exists, conservative fertility-preserving management options do exist. Diagnosing ovarian pregnancies solely through TVUS imaging remains difficult due to the similarities between hemorrhagic cysts and ectopic pregnancies; therefore, histopathological confirmation based on Spiegelberg’s criteria is often required (Please refer to the Histopathology section for more information regarding Spiegelberg’s criteria). [11] Rare cases of abdominal pregnancies, which may involve implantation in the retroperitoneal space, liver, spleen, appendix, or even lung, are frequently identified only during surgical intervention. Clinical signs, eg, fetal malpresentation, oligohydramnios, and elevated maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein levels, can provide diagnostic clues. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 544
retrieved_docs.content: Laparoscopy (gi only) is the central minimally invasive surgical modality for a variety of gynecologic procedures. Specific operations that are classified as direct descendants or instances of this laparoscopic approach include Laparoscopic lysis of adhesions, Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, bilateral, Laparoscopic salpingectomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, Laparoscopic salpingostomy, Laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, and Laparoscopy with removal of adnexal structures. These procedures are integral to the management of ectopic pregnancy; they fall under the hierarchical category Procedures for management of ectopic pregnancy, which itself is a subcategory of removal of ectopic pregnancy. Within this framework, Fetotoxic injection of ectopic pregnancy via laparoscopy and Laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy are identified as child procedures of the ectopic‑pregnancy management group, while Laparoscopic salpingectomy with removal of tubal pregnancy is directly mapped as a primary procedure for removal of ectopic pregnancy. Clinically, laparoscopy (gi only) frequently co‑occurs with conditions such as التواء المبيض (ovarian torsion), خراج بوقي مبيضي (tubo‑ovarian abscess), and فَغْرُ البُوْق (tubal rupture), indicating that these acute gynecologic emergencies often necessitate laparoscopic intervention. Recognizing the hierarchical relationships among these laparoscopic techniques and their association with ectopic pregnancy management and related gynecologic pathologies supports comprehensive patient care and informed surgical planning.

Procedures for management of ectopic pregnancy are organized under the broader umbrella of removal of ectopic pregnancy. Within this hierarchy, laparoscopic salpingectomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, and fetotoxic injection of ectopic pregnancy via laparoscopy are identified as concrete child procedures. The fetotoxic injection represents an alternative, minimally invasive method for terminating tubal pregnancies. Abdominal surgeries encompass a wide range of procedures, including the fallopian tube surgeries described above, and are frequently reported together with pelvic inflammatory disease and peritonitis, reflecting common comorbidities in patients undergoing

Abdominal surgeries co‑occur with الْتِهابُ الصِّفاقِ (peritonitis) and are inversely related to fallopian tube surgeries, underscoring that tubal surgeries constitute a subset of abdominal procedures. Collectively, these relationships highlight the central role of the fallopian tubes in ectopic pregnancy management, inflammatory pelvic disease, and related laparoscopic interventions, emphasizing the need for precise procedural mapping and appropriate qualifiers to optimize patient care.

Infectious processes are identified as the pathological basis of post‑abortion complications, underscoring infection control in post‑abortion care (إجْهاضٌ مَصْحُوبٌ بعَدْوَى). Laparoscopic treatment of ectopic pregnancy without salpingectomy or oophorectomy illustrates conservative minimally invasive management of ectopic and abdominal pregnancies and remains linked to fetal monitoring, reflecting continued fetal assessment. Postpartum obstetric operations are associated with immediate postpartum care and investigation (Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling). Collectively, إجراء توليدي serves as the central hierarchical category that integrates ante‑ and postpartum obstetric interventions—from diagnostic punctures and fetal monitoring to major surgical operations—within a framework

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2911
retrieved_docs.content: Laparoscopic treatment of ectopic pregnancy; without salpingectomy and/or oophorectomy is a specific form of surgical management that is directly related to the broader category of surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy; abdominal pregnancy. This minimally invasive approach addresses ectopic implantation while preserving the fallopian tube and ovary, distinguishing it from more radical procedures that involve removal of these structures. By maintaining reproductive anatomy, the laparoscopic technique offers an alternative to traditional abdominal surgical interventions for ectopic pregnancies, highlighting its relevance within the spectrum of operative options for this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 487
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures for management of ectopic pregnancy encompass several laparoscopic interventions: fetotoxic injection of ectopic pregnancy via laparoscopy, laparoscopic salpingectomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, and laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy. These interventions, together with laparoscopic lysis of adhesions and removal of adnexal structures, underscore the central role of laparoscopy and the pelvic organ vejcovody in diagnosing, treating, and planning care for ectopic pregnancy, ovarian torsion, tubo‑ovarian and pelvic pouch abscesses, pelvic inflammatory disease, and related pelvic inflammatory complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1853
retrieved_docs.content: Fetotoxic injection of ectopic pregnancy via laparoscopy and laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy are both specific procedural approaches employed in the management of ectopic pregnancy. These two techniques are considered child procedures under the broader category of procedures for management of ectopic pregnancy, illustrating minimally invasive options for treating ectopic gestations. The fetotoxic injection method utilizes a chemical agent delivered laparoscopically to terminate the ectopic pregnancy, while the laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy involves surgically opening the fallopian tube and extracting the ectopic tissue. Both procedures reflect contemporary strategies aimed at preserving fertility and reducing morbidity associated with ectopic pregnancy treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 644
retrieved_docs.content: The management of ectopic pregnancy is organized as a hierarchy of procedures. The overarching category “Procedures for management of ectopic pregnancy” is derived from the broader concept of “removal of ectopic pregnancy.” Within this category, specific laparoscopic techniques are employed, including the fetotoxic injection of ectopic pregnancy via laparoscopy and the laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, both of which are child procedures of the management category. Additionally, the laparoscopic salpingectomy with removal of tubal pregnancy is directly mapped from the removal of ectopic pregnancy, representing another definitive surgical option. Together, these interventions illustrate the range of minimally invasive strategies used to excise ectopic gestations and preserve patient health.

The infectious process is identified as the underlying mechanism of post‑abortion complications, linking infection directly to adverse outcomes after termination procedures. Collectively, this structured framework interrelates prenatal surveillance, invasive diagnostics, therapeutic interventions, delivery assistance, placental and ectopic‑pregnancy management, and post‑delivery care, providing a comprehensive reference for clinical documentation, education, and patient‑centered obstetric management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: pelvic inflammatory disease, and tubal‑ovarian abscess, while ectopic pregnancy is clinically associated with laparotomy (شق البطن) when open surgical access is required. Moreover, abdominal surgeries, including fallopian tube operations, are commonly linked with peritoneal inflammation (التهاب الصفاق), underscoring the need for preventive and postoperative monitoring to reduce this complication. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how salpingectomy and its laparoscopic variants integrate anatomical knowledge of the fallopian tubes with the diagnosis and surgical management of ectopic pregnancy, tubal infections, and related pelvic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1998
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions performed during gestation, including the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy; abdominal pregnancy and the procedure referred to as بضع الفرج. The surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy; abdominal pregnancy is identified as a child of Pregnancy-Related Procedures and is linked to a specific minimally invasive option: laparoscopic treatment of ectopic pregnancy; without salpingectomy and/or oophorectomy. This laparoscopic approach represents a distinct relationship within the broader category of surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy; abdominal pregnancy, offering a technique that preserves the fallopian tube and ovary while addressing the ectopic implantation. Together, these concepts illustrate how various pregnancy‑related procedures are organized hierarchically and how advanced laparoscopic methods can be integrated into the management of abdominal ectopic pregnancies.

A fetotoxic injection of an ectopic pregnancy via laparoscopy is also mapped to laparoscopy (gi only), reflecting the use of this minimally invasive approach for ectopic‑pregnancy management. Removal of ectopic pregnancy is inversely related to salpingectomy, indicating that salpingectomy constitutes one principal method for achieving ectopic‑pregnancy removal. Laparoscopy (gi only) co‑occurs with tubo‑ovarian abscess (خراج بوقي مبيضي) and is frequently mentioned together with فَغْرُ البُوْق (the fallopian tube), underscoring its clinical relevance to tubal pathology. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1154
retrieved_docs.content: Laparoscopy (GI only) is a minimally invasive category of abdominal surgery that serves as a parent concept for a wide range of gynecologic and general‑intestinal operations, including laparoscopic salpingostomy, unilateral or bilateral laparoscopic salpingectomy, laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, laparoscopic salpingectomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, laparoscopic removal of adnexal structures, laparoscopic lysis of adhesions, and related techniques. These procedures are classified as child procedures of “Procedures for management of ectopic pregnancy,” which itself is a subcategory of the broader “removal of ectopic pregnancy.” A fetotoxic injection of an ectopic pregnancy performed via laparoscopy is also placed within this management pathway. Clinically, ectopic pregnancy often necessitates laparotomy and may co‑occur with complications such as tubal rupture, ovarian (tubal) abscess, and other pelvic pathology, all of which are frequently addressed with laparoscopic interventions. The laparoscopy (GI only) node is hierarchically linked to percutaneous nephrolithotomy, reflecting its relevance to certain urologic procedures, and it is positioned under the Arabic‑language category “العمليات الجراحية في البطن” (abdominal surgeries). Abdominal surgeries (“العمليات الجراحية في البطن”) show a recurrent association with postoperative peritonitis (الْتِهابُ الصِّفاقِ), indicating that patients undergoing procedures within the abdominal cavity are at heightened risk for this complication. Surgeries of the fallopian tubes (“عمليات جراحية لقناة فالوب”) are specifically categorized as a subset of abdominal surgeries, reinforcing the relationship between tubal procedures, the broader class of abdominal operative care, and the associated peritonitis risk. Recognizing these interconnections aids in risk assessment, peri‑operative planning, and post‑surgical care for patients undergoing laparoscopic, tubal, or other abdominal interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: استئصال قناة فالوب is a surgical intervention that belongs to the broader category of Female Reproductive System Procedures. It is performed in two specific laparoscopic forms: Laparoscopic salpingectomy, bilateral and Laparoscopic salpingectomy, unilateral, both of which are child procedures of استئصال قناة فالوب. Clinically, استئصال قناة فالوب is associated with حمل منتبذ, indicating a relationship between fallopian tube removal and the occurrence or management of ectopic pregnancy. In addition, استئصال قناة فالوب frequently co‑occurs with كيسة مبيضية, reflecting that patients undergoing this procedure often have concurrent ovarian cysts. Recognizing these hierarchical and clinical connections supports comprehensive evaluation and optimal care planning for patients requiring fallopian tube surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 612
retrieved_docs.content: Laparoscopy (gi only) serves as a procedural platform for a variety of minimally invasive interventions that are employed in the management of ectopic pregnancy and related adnexal pathology. Specific techniques that are mapped from laparoscopy (gi only) include Fetotoxic injection of ectopic pregnancy via laparoscopy, Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, bilateral, Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, unilateral, Laparoscopic salpingectomy, bilateral, Laparoscopic salpingectomy, unilateral, Laparoscopic salpingectomy with removal of tubal pregnancy, and Laparoscopic salpingotomy with removal of tubal pregnancy. These procedures are classified as children of the broader category Procedures for management of ectopic pregnancy, which itself is a child of the overarching concept removal of ectopic pregnancy. In addition, Laparoscopy with removal of adnexal structures is identified as a type of laparoscopy (gi only). Clinical observations indicate that laparoscopy (gi only) frequently co‑occurs with كيسة مبيضية (ovarian cyst) and مرض التهاب الحوض (pelvic inflammatory disease), reflecting the common coexistence of these gynecologic conditions in patients undergoing laparoscopic evaluation or treatment. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how laparoscopic techniques are integrated into the therapeutic algorithm for ectopic pregnancy, offering options ranging from chemical termination (fetotoxic injection) to surgical excision of the affected fallopian tube (salpingectomy, partial salpingectomy, salpingotomy), while also addressing concurrent adnexal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.
